category_title: Six-year-old students with EBD, ADHD, and ODD who exhibit noncompliance, aggression, and disrobing during difficult academic tasks
minimal_context: Axel is sitting in his individual work area being asked to complete a multistep academic worksheet.
dialogue:
TUTOR: Axel, it is time for your worksheet. First, write your name at the top, and then circle the pictures.

STUDENT: No! I'm not doing it!

TUTOR: You need to follow the directions and do your work.

STUDENT: (Throws his pencil out of the work area) It's too hard!

TUTOR: Please stop throwing things and start your assignment.

STUDENT: (Screams a swear word, removes his shoes and socks, and makes an obscene gesture at the tutor)

options.A: Review the consequence maps every morning before the first work session. Give Axel the clipboard containing the maps and calm down schedule, letting him decide which to listen to first. Read the negative behaviors and consequences, followed by the positive ones.
options.B: Use various pockets and holders to keep the academic worksheet, pencils, and other supplies contained within Axel's individual work area, desk, or book bag throughout the duration of the morning work session.
options.C: Engage Axel in alternative hands-on activities such as drawing, painting, water coloring, and pottery making in his individual work area before transitioning to the academic worksheet during the morning work session.
options.D: Provide the academic worksheets using large fonts with supporting pictures, reduce the number of questions to answer, allow access to word banks, and incorporate speech-generating devices during the work session.
